stories.seo_title: Why birds bob their heads — it is not what you think
stories.yt_description: Birds bob their heads when they walk. It’s not about balance—it’s about vision. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Birds don’t walk—they snap. That head bob isn’t for balance, it’s for vision. When they move, their eyes lock on prey while their body catches up. stories.description: A clip of farm animals: Ali Akdemir — pexels
stories.seo_title: Why farm animals have stomachs you won't believe 🐑
stories.yt_description: Farm animals have a stomach design that evolved over 50 million years. A cow's stomach can hold up to 50 gallons of food and liquid. Source: Pexels
stories.script: Cows have four stomachs. They’re not separate organs, but one stomach with four compartments. The first one, the rumen, ferments food before it’s chewed again—yep, they burp up their lunch to chew it a second time. Chickens have something even weirder: they swallow rocks to grind their food. Ever seen a chicken pecking at gravel? Now you know why. So, which farm animal’s digestion blew your mind more?

stories.script: Animals yawn to cool their brains. A big yawn can drop a monkey’s brain temp by almost two degrees. Horses yawn before fights to signal they’re ready, not sleepy. Even fish yawn—but only when oxygen runs low. So next time you see an animal yawn, ask yourself: is it really tired? Or just managing its brain like a thermostat?
